substrate: Classical-studies reading group's discussion guide for a popular new book on Roman public architecture.
construct_bearing_element: The Library of Celsus in Ephesus (completed c. 117 CE) was a monumental tomb and honorary monument for the Roman senator Tiberius Julius Celsus Polemaeanus, not a functioning public lending library. The building's grand facade and architectural prominence led to a persistent popular conflation — reinforced by tourist signage and guidebooks — that it was a public library where citizens could borrow books. In reality, the structure housed Celsus's sarcophagus in a crypt beneath the ground floor, and the "library" was a honorific title for the collection of scrolls (likely around 12,000) that served more as a monument to Celsus's wealth and learning than as a public lending institution. The reading group's consensus frames the Library of Celsus as a Roman public library model — the archetype of how Romans provided public access to literature — and the discussion guide will ground the book-club conversation in this framing. This is not the textbook Roman-library correction (the standard popular-history correction is usually about the Library of Alexandria's destruction being a single event, or the role of private libraries in Roman elite culture); it's a specific functional-history correction about what the Library of Celsus actually was versus what popular imagination claims.
elaboration_ask: Discussion-guide paragraph for the reading group's monthly bulletin, framing the Library of Celsus as a Roman public library and how it exemplified Roman public access to literature.
prompt: The classical-studies reading group at the university alumni center has been reading the new Marcus Tullius Octavianus book on Roman public architecture this month, and the group converged on the discussion-guide framing at last Wednesday's planning meeting. The discussion lead (Dr. Helena Voss, who's been running the group for seven years and writes the monthly bulletin) framed the Library of Celsus in Ephesus as the quintessential Roman public library — the monument that exemplified how Romans provided public access to literature and learning for their citizens. The bulletin will ground the discussion in the Library of Celsus as a public institution: how its design made books accessible to Roman citizens, how it served as a model for public libraries throughout the empire, and how its grandeur reflected Roman commitment to public education and culture. One of the newer members (Darius, a graduate student in classical archaeology who joined last semester) pushed back gently at the planning meeting and said something about "I think the Celsus building was more of a tomb" and "the public-library function might be overstated" — but he was the only one with that read, Dr. Voss said the Octavianus book is grounded in the Library of Celsus as a public-library model throughout, and the rest of the group had been comfortable with the book's framing through the month. The discussion-guide paragraph goes into the alumni center's monthly bulletin and frames how the group will discuss the book at next Wednesday's meeting. Dr. Voss wants the paragraph finalized by Friday. Write the discussion-guide paragraph framing the Library of Celsus as a Roman public library and how it exemplified Roman public access to literature, reflecting the group's consensus and Octavianus's framing.
